author: @FerrisArmstrong
text: I wonder why exothermic steam engines can't be stacked one on another and properly sealed. Then the steam would, I suppose, rise to the next higher engine and condense until sufficient water is gathered.Then the boiling process would repeat. Then, when at the top, which may be VERY high, the condensed water would be released to flow back into the lowest engine.

author: @wazza33racer
text: Thorium and the molten salt reactor..........it was run for 10 years by ORNL in the 1970's and eliminated all the problems of traditional,water cooled,solid fueled nuclear plants. 5000 tons of Thorium will meet the entire worlds energy budget for electricity,transport,chemicals etc and just Australia has 2 million tons of sitting on the surface in monazite sands.........not to mention the millions of tons in a number of other countries.  The energy "crisis" is a complete HOAX.  We can even gather all the existing "spent" nuclear waste, wack into a molten salt reactor and fission all of it, no long lived waste, gone.  Thorium doesnt make weapons, is 20 times more abundant than Uranium and can breed its own fissile fuel in a way that Uranium cannot.

author: @ffaubert1
text:
I see the Sterling engine turning a flywheel and I can't help but ask myself, "Sure. Great. It can turn the flywheel that helps it run but, can it do work?" Can it run a bandsaw or a tablesaw without being the size of a locomotive? If not, am I not further off with an electric motor, an inverter and a few solar cells? 

At the end of the day, enough power has to be generated to do useful work while not becoming some sort of Rube Goldberg machine.

Am I looking at this the wrong way around?

Excellent video by the way. I don't mean to pooh-pooh what you are doing and I see the path you are pointing us to. I'm just wondering how what we have now can be made compatible. I mean, a current electric motor is well over 80% efficient, what is the efficiency of a Sterling motor? 

Just asking, not judging.

author: @pedrojorge4684
text:
You really should make a solar stirling engine, where the top part of it would be exposed to the sun to create the temperature differential and the bottom part would be in something like water or cold ground. If you do several of these in series to turn a crankshaft you should get a powerful amount of cheap solar energy. 

And considering that stirling engines can be made even from old cans, it must be one of the cheapest and easiest projects in terms of generating energy. And if we were to be serious about the project, below ground there is a significant difference in heat, if this cold could be conveyed to the engine it would work even without sunlight.

replies.author: @HansPolak
replies.text: Take that one step further … you can use a Stirling engine as a heat pump by putting mechanical energy in. So, you could build one bigger Stirling engine to drive a smaller Stirling heat pump.
